request.question: Scott Howell is a consultant who has worked with the mayor of what city?

title: David Cruz Thayne
David Morgan (psychoanalyst)
Matilda Howell
Rudy Giuliani
Jun Choi
Pete Ashdown
Howell School
Scott Howell (footballer)
Scott Howell (political consultant)
United States Senate election in Utah, 2012
sentences: David Cruz Thayne (March 10, 1971) is an American small businessman, former professional tennis player, and Democratic congressional candidate.

He formerly served as chief of staff and campaign manager to Utah State Senate Minority Leader Scott Howell, a Democrat, and currently operates his own tennis-oriented small business in Southern California which has also led to the production of two tennis oriented documentaries.
He was a candidate for California’s 26th Congressional District in the United States House of Representatives elections in California, 2012.
David Howell Morgan (BAHons D.Clin.Psych.
FBPAS FPBPA SMBPF.
Training Analyst Supervisor IPA.
AMBPS.
MBPC) is a psychoanalyst and consultant psychotherapist who has worked at the Portman Clinic London for 25 years.
He went on to specialise in patients' presenting with problems around sexuality and violence.
Morgan co-edited a popular textbook on sexuality, perversion and delinquency.
He is now a psychoanalyst and consultant seeing private patients and provides consultations to political groups.
He is chair of the Political Minds Seminars at the British Psychoanalytic Society.
He regularly contributes to radio and television and lectures nationally and internationally.

Morgan’s recent research has focused on the psychological implications for whistleblowers and the conditions, socially, politically, and psychologically under which a person may "turn a blind eye" or take on the responsibility of whistleblowing.
He also presents a podcast called "Frontier Psychoanalyst" on BBC i-player.
Lida Scott Howell (August 28, 1859 – December 20, 1938) was an American female archer who competed in the early twentieth century.
She won three gold medals in Archery at the 1904 Summer Olympics in Missouri in the double national and Columbia rounds and for the US team.

Rudolph William Louis Giuliani {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} ( ; born May 28, 1944) is an American lawyer, businessman, public speaker, former mayor of New York City, and an informal adviser on cybersecurity to the White House.
Jun H. Choi (born May 17, 1971) is an American politician and the former Mayor of Edison, New Jersey, a community of over 100,000 people and the fifth largest municipality in the state.
He was sworn in on January 1, 2006 as the youngest mayor in Edison history and the first Asian American mayor of a major city in New Jersey.
He began a good government, reform movement that led to greater fiscal responsibility, economic development, public education improvements, environmental protection and civic engagement.
Prior to becoming Mayor of Edison, Choi worked in both business and government.

He served as a senior official on education policy at the New Jersey Department of Education where he started the NJ SMART Program, improving public school education performance metrics for 1.4 million New Jersey children.
He also served in the White House Office of Management and Budget and worked as a management consultant focusing on strategy and technology issues for Fortune 500 US corporations.
Peter Lynn "Pete" Ashdown (born January 11, 1967) is the founder and CEO of Utah's first independent and oldest Internet service provider, XMission.
Running as the Democratic candidate in 2006, Ashdown lost to incumbent U.S. Senator Orrin Hatch.
Ashdown ran again in 2012 for the U.S. Senate, but was defeated in his pursuit of the Democratic nomination at the state convention by Scott Howell.
Howell School is a historic school building in Dothan, Alabama.
The school was built in 1902 to replace the previous grammar school that had burned in 1901.
It was named in 1916 for the recently deceased Dr. Robert Graves Howell, who was mayor when the first grammar school was built.
A second school was built in 1911, and Howell was replaced by Minnie T. Heard Elementary School in 1942.
After being used by the Salvation Army during World War II, the building was converted for use as a textile mill in 1947.
A number of companies operated out of the building until 1952, when the Dothan Manufacturing Company took over the property.
A one-story addition was constructed on the front of the building in 1965, obscuring the façade.
The plant closed in 1997, and was sold to the Southern Alabama Regional Council on the Aging in 2003, before the city re-acquired it in 2008.
Scott Howell (born 10 May 1958) is a former Australian rules footballer who played with Carlton in the VFL during the early 1980s.
Herbert Weston Scott Howell III is an American conservative political consultant, whose recent clients include Meg Whitman and Rudy Giuliani.

The 2012 United States Senate election in Utah took place on November 6, 2012, concurrently with the 2012 U.S. presidential election as well as other elections to the United States Senate and House of Representatives and as various state and local elections.
Incumbent Republican U.S. Senator Orrin Hatch won re-election to a seventh term against former state Senator and IBM executive, Scott Howell the Democratic candidate.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: Scott Howell is a consultant who has worked with the mayor of what city?
hop1_docs: Scott Howell (political consultant) | Herbert Weston Scott Howell III is an American conservative political consultant, whose recent clients include Meg Whitman and Rudy Giuliani.

Scott Smith (American politician) | Scott Smith (born 1956) is an American businessman and politician, elected as the 38th mayor of Mesa, Arizona, on May 20, 2008.  He took office on June 2, 2008 and served until April 16, 2014, when he resigned to run for Governor of Arizona in the Republican Primary.  In 2013-14, he was president of the United States Conference of Mayors.  He was previously president of both Great Western Homes and K. Hovnanian Homes.  He has also worked in finance and as a business consultant.
Scott Howell (footballer) | Scott Howell (born 10 May 1958) is a former Australian rules footballer who played with Carlton in the VFL during the early 1980s.

Scott Howell (politician) | Scott N. Howell (born September 28, 1953) is an American politician and Utah who was a candidate for the United States Senate in the 2012 election.  He is a member of the Democratic Party.

Municipality of Isfahan | The municipality of Isfahan is a non-governmental organization, which is responsible for the management of the city of Isfahan.  The highest-ranking official of this organization is the mayor of Isfahan, who is chosen by the city council of Isfahan.  The city of Isfahan has 15 administrative divisions and each division has its own mayor, who work under the mayor of Isfahan.  The present mayor of Isfahan is Mehdi Jamalinejad.

City Mayors Foundation | The City Mayors Foundation, also known as City Mayors, is an international think tank dedicated to urban affairs.  It has been active since 2003 and runs the biennial World Mayor award, as well as providing pro bono consultancy services.  Unlike Eurocities and United Cities and Local Governments it is wholly independent of any city.

W. Ray Scott | Walter Ray Scott, Sr. (March 19, 1923 – February 23, 2008), usually known as W. Ray Scott, was from 1960 to 1976 the Democratic mayor of Natchitoches, the oldest city in Louisiana.  As mayor, he is primarily remembered for his work with the administration of Governor Edwin Washington Edwards in procuring physical expansion and university status for Northwestern State University, formerly Northwestern State College.  In addition, he worked to establish the municipal water supply from Sibley Lake in Natchitoches.  From 1956 to 1960, Scott was the elected municipal finance commissioner.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Scott Howell (political consultant) and his client Rudy Giuliani.

- "Herbert Weston Scott Howell III" — identified as "an American conservative political consultant" (passage [1]).
- Passage [1] lists "recent clients include Meg Whitman and Rudy Giuliani." (quoted verbatim)
- Ambiguity / missing fact: passages do not state which city Rudy Giuliani served as mayor — missing fact: "mayoral city" for bridge entity "Rudy Giuliani".
- Name-variant ambiguity: other Scott Howells appear in the passages ("Scott N. Howell (politician)"; "Scott Howell (footballer)"); ensure retrieval targets the political consultant.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. "Rudy Giuliani" — fetch lede/infobox and "Political career"/"Mayor" sections to identify the city he served as mayor.
2. "Scott Howell (political consultant)" — fetch lede/infobox and "Clients" or "Career" sections to confirm the consultant–Giuliani relationship.
3. Search keyword if needed: "Scott Howell Rudy Giuliani consultant 'Herbert Weston Scott Howell III'".
4. If Giuliani page is ambiguous, fetch "Rudy Giuliani (disambiguation)" or contemporaneous news profiles linking Giuliani to his mayoral role.
hop2_docs: Mayoralty of Rudy Giuliani | Rudy Giuliani (full name "Rudolph William Louis Giuliani") served as the 107th Mayor of New York City from January 1, 1994 until December 31, 2001.
Electoral history of Rudy Giuliani | Electoral history of Rudy Giuliani, 107th Mayor of New York City and was a candidate for the 2008 Republican presidential nomination.

Rudy Giuliani promotions of Bernard Kerik | Rudy Giuliani, as mayor of the City of New York, promoted the career of Bernard Kerik (by appointment or recommendation) on several occasions.  Critics have charged that Giuliani showed consistently poor judgment in doing so.
Rudy Washington | Rudy Washington is an American politician who served as deputy mayor under New York City former mayor Rudy Giuliani.
Giuliani Time | Giuliani Time is a 2005 documentary film by Kevin Keating about Rudy Giuliani, former Mayor of New York City.
Helen Giuliani | Helen Giuliani (née D'Avanzo; September 26, 1909 – September 8 2002) was the mother of former New York City Mayor Rudy Giuliani.

Rudy Giuliani: Urban Legend | Rudy Giuliani: Urban Legend is a video produced by the International Association of Firefighters (IAFF).  On July 11, 2007, the IAFF released the 13-minute video in DVD format to fire departments across the U.S.  The DVD outlines its complaints against Rudy Giuliani.  It is critical of the 2008 Republican Party presidential candidate and former New York City mayor.  As the video has been issued on a website, and not just DVD, it is classifiable as a viral video.
hop2_titles: Rudy Giuliani promotions of Bernard Kerik
response.summary_2: Scott Howell has worked with the mayor of New York City. Rudy Giuliani served as the 107th Mayor of New York City from January 1, 1994 to December 31, 2001.
